segments.title: Trafalgar, invasion pressure, and French commerce raiding
segments.summary: A Trafalgar question separates French commerce-raiding options from the fleet concentration needed to contest the Channel.
segments.body: The answer treats Trafalgar as likely once the French and Spanish fleets tried to clear the Channel for invasion. French commerce raiding remains a viable alternative in smaller units, but it points to a different strategy: harassing trade rather than forcing a decisive passage for an invasion fleet.

evidence.note: The question asks whether Trafalgar was inevitable and whether French commerce raiding could work if major fleet battles were avoided.

segments.question: Was Trafalgar inevitable, and could the French conduct successful commerce raiding if they avoided major sea battles?
segments.answerShort: Commerce raiding was possible, but a Channel invasion strategy required fleet concentration and made a major battle likely.

segments.title: A 23-knot British battle line and German design pressure
segments.summary: A counterfactual Orion question shows how faster British battleships could make Germany's naval race more expensive and more constrained.
segments.body: The answer frames speed as a way to raise the cost of competing battleships, pushing German naval construction into harder budget tradeoffs with the army. It also connects the 23-knot battle line to larger later British designs, where the panel expects much bigger ships and possibly earlier Admiral-class style thinking.

evidence.note: The answer says a 23-knot British battle line would make German ships more expensive and force sacrifices elsewhere.
The panel returns to the same counterfactual and expects much larger follow-on ships.

segments.question: If the 23-knot Orion preliminary design had been selected and following battleships matched that speed, how would Germany respond?
segments.answerShort: Germany would face larger, more expensive designs and sharper tradeoffs as Britain raised the cost of matching the battle line.

segments.title: Sharing fire-control data when a ship's own system is damaged
segments.summary: A fire-control question compares mechanical-era fallbacks with modern data-link requirements for another ship directing weapons.
segments.body: The answer distinguishes older mechanical systems, where data could be displayed, signalled, or backed up by turret sighting and manual control, from modern ships that need compatible data links. The useful point is that cross-ship fire control is not magic improvisation: it depends on built-in arrangements, surviving local equipment, and the period's communication tools.

evidence.note: The answer describes fire-control clocks, signalling, manual aiming, and turret sighting as older fallback options.
For modern ships, the answer says cross-ship control would require data links built in advance.

segments.question: How can one ship transmit fire-control data to another ship if the receiving ship has its own system shut out?
segments.answerShort: Older ships could fall back on displayed or signalled data and manual control; modern ships need pre-planned data-link integration.

segments.title: Landlocked states and blue-water warships
segments.summary: A quick force-structure answer distinguishes landlocked gunboat forces from true blue-water warships.
segments.body: The answer rejects the idea that a landlocked country currently fields blue-water warships, while noting that river or lake gunboats are a different category. Paraguay appears as the edge case, useful for understanding why geographic access and operating area matter as much as nominal naval ownership.

evidence.note: The answer says there is probably no current landlocked state with blue-water warships.
The follow-up distinguishes Paraguay's gunboats from blue-water warships.

segments.question: Is there any landlocked country today that has blue-water warships?
segments.answerShort: Probably not; river or brown-water gunboats do not make a blue-water force.

segments.title: Choosing between Yorktown, Shokaku, and Illustrious
segments.summary: A carrier-choice question turns on theatre, land-based air threat, machinery layout, and aircraft sustainment.
segments.body: The answer favors Illustrious in the Mediterranean and Yorktown in the Atlantic or Pacific, showing how the best carrier depends on where it must fight. Shokaku is respected, but the answer points to machinery-space arrangement and aircraft sustainment assumptions tied to Japanese plans for an attritional Pacific campaign.

evidence.note: The answer chooses between Yorktown, Shokaku, and Illustrious based on theatre and air-threat environment.
The answer critiques Shokaku machinery-space weighting and aircraft sustainment assumptions.

segments.question: If you had to be on a Yorktown, Shokaku, or Illustrious class carrier, which one would you pick?
segments.answerShort: Illustrious is preferred for the Mediterranean, while Yorktown is favored for Atlantic and Pacific contexts.

segments.title: Defence Investment Plan gaps and the time needed to rebuild
segments.summary: A current-affairs question asks how long Britain would need to correct Defence Investment Plan weaknesses and rebuild defence capacity.
segments.body: The answer refuses a simple timetable because implementation depends on whether later leaders preserve the plan, alter it, and fund it. It also highlights why the thinking behind the plan matters even if ministerial leadership changes: the officials and assumptions shaping procurement may remain in place.

evidence.note: The question asks how long it would take to correct Defence Investment Plan deficiencies and rebuild required defence levels.
The answer says the plan's value depends on implementation and how much later teams change or retain it.

segments.question: How long would it take to correct deficiencies in the Defence Investment Plan and rebuild the defence levels the country requires?
segments.answerShort: There is no quick timetable; the answer depends on political continuity, implementation, and real funding.

segments.title: Whether Grace Dieu is worth raising
segments.summary: A maritime-archaeology question asks whether enough of Grace Dieu survives to justify raising and preserving her.
segments.body: The answer is yes from the perspective of a naval historian, because recovering and preserving the ship would open valuable study opportunities. It also carries an important caveat: a marine archaeologist might judge the preservation tradeoffs differently, so the historical appeal is not the only consideration.

evidence.note: The question asks whether enough of Grace Dieu survives to make raising her worthwhile; the answer says yes with an archaeology caveat.

segments.question: Does enough of Grace Dieu survive to make raising her worthwhile?
segments.answerShort: Probably yes for naval-history study, though marine-archaeology preservation concerns could change that assessment.

segments.title: World War I dreadnoughts against a World War II King George V
segments.summary: A battleship comparison asks how many German World War I dreadnoughts could seriously threaten a World War II King George V.
segments.body: The answer makes range the key variable: older heavy shells remain dangerous if the ships can close enough to hit. It then broadens the counterfactual by noting that replacing whole force structures with 1940s counterparts creates a very different and lopsided scenario, not a clean one-for-one duel.

evidence.note: The answer says World War I heavy shells remain dangerous if the dreadnoughts get close enough.
The answer expands the replacement scenario to later battlecruisers, destroyers, and modernized older battleships.

segments.question: How many World War I German dreadnoughts would be a significant threat to a World War II King George V?
segments.answerShort: They become dangerous if they close the range, but the wider counterfactual changes whole force structures rather than one ship.

segments.title: Axis response to a Mosquito-mounted bouncing bomb
segments.summary: A weapons counterfactual asks how Axis navies would react if Britain fielded a Mosquito-mounted bouncing bomb against battleships.
segments.body: The answer says the reaction depends first on whether the weapon works and achieves visible success. Italy would have more battleships at risk, while Germany would likely have even more reason to keep major surface units sheltered and use them as a fleet-in-being rather than an active striking fleet.

evidence.note: The answer says the counter depends on whether Britain achieves operational success with the weapon.
The answer says Italy has more battleships at risk while Germany would be pushed further toward sheltering its surface fleet.

segments.question: What would the Axis response have been if Britain used a Mosquito-mounted bouncing bomb against German battleships?
segments.answerShort: Success would force reactions; Italy has more capital ships exposed, while Germany would likely shelter its surface fleet even more.
